answer: FINAL ANSWER: Below is a concise, evidence‑backed diagnosis and an action plan you can run now. I map each of your numbered symptoms to the most likely recorded cause(s), list the immediate artifacts to collect, and give the recommended priority / support level. I don’t invent anything — where I can match your symptom to the incident records I show the supporting references. Executive summary - The pattern and timing strongly match a cross‑service, weekend change at the tenant/perimeter (firewall/WAF/NAT or tenant security defaults) that produced multiple, concurrent failures across D365 Financial Reporting, Exchange/SMTP, Teams/Direct‑Routing, SBC/trunk routing and printing. The incident materials treat this as a high‑impact, multi‑service outage and recommend 2nd‑level escalation to Network/UC, Identity and Infrastructure owners

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 93: # Perimeter Firewall, Reverse‑Proxy/WAF & Voice NAT (Zurich/Basel) This community centers on perimeter and site firewalls, a reverse‑proxy/WAF, NAT rules for published services, and an externally‑accessible FTP footprint that was decommissioned. A weekend security patch and an emergency security‑hardening change caused template/profile reloads and an outbound restriction: the perimeter change activated a default inspection profile (Default‑Strict) on the reverse‑proxy/WAF which replaced a custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling producing 502s; separately, an outbound restriction at the Zurich edge blocked RTP media to the SBC and produced no audio for external calls. Basel’s site showed a standard port configuration and correct port 443/NAT entries, pointing to NAT/state timeout interactions rather than simple port blocking as the cause of voice degradation. An employee‑recorded removal of a firewall policy on 17.12.24 resulted in decommissioning of externally‑accessible FTP. The corpus provides logs and change links that correlate these configuration changes with observed service failures. [Data: Entities (303, 310, 336, 304, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 502, 541, 1095)] ## Reverse‑proxy/WAF profile reload (Default‑Strict) replaced custom template and caused header handling regressions and 502s A security patch/reload resulted in the default inspection/profile "Default‑Strict" being activated on the reverse‑proxy/WAF, which replaced the previously used custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling; this change is directly linked by ticket notes to 502 errors and header removal observed after the patch [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. The records show a template/profile-level change on the reverse‑proxy/WAF rather than an application‑level code change, indicating configuration drift at the perimeter layer is responsible for application‑facing HTTP errors; this makes template management and configuration rollback capability primary risk/mitigation points for restoring service quickly after future patches [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. ## Emergency outbound restriction at Zurich blocked RTP to SBC and caused no audio for external calls Investigation by the Network Team identified an emergency security‑hardening change at the Zurich edge that introduced a new outbound restriction blocking client RTP ports to the Session Border Controller (SBC), which produced no audio for users calling external parties [Data: Entities (303)]. Firewall logs corroborated the timing and presence of the outbound restriction rule, supporting a causal link between the firewall change and the onset of audio issues; the logs were pivotal in correlating change time to the incident start time [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. ## Basel site: standard port rules present; NAT and session/timeout behavior implicated rather than port blocking Checks at the Basel site validated the standard port configuration and confirmed that port 443 rules and NAT entries were present and correct, demonstrating that simple port closures were not the cause of service regression there [Data: Entities (336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. Because ports and NAT entries appeared correct, the analysis points to NAT/state timeout interactions (or asymmetric NAT behaviour) as contributing factors to voice impact, indicating a need to review NAT timeouts and session persistence for Voice VLAN/NAT policies to prevent intermittent media failures under patched or hardened firewall states [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (502, 541)]. ## Perimeter firewall patch produced configuration drift affecting reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior The perimeter firewall received a security patch/upgrade over the same weekend that—despite logs not showing obvious drops—overwrote or altered templates/policies and caused configuration drift that affected reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior; this is noted explicitly in the firewall analysis summary [Data: Entities (303)]. The combination of silent template/policy changes on perimeter firewalls and their downstream effects on WAF/reverse‑proxy headers creates a high‑impact failure mode because application layer failures (502s, header loss) can appear unrelated to the original network/security change without clear cross‑team change visibility [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (497)]. ## External FTP decommissioning was performed by an identified employee removing the governing firewall policy An externally‑accessible FTP service footprint was decommissioned on 17.12.24 when the firewall policy governing it was removed; ticket evidence records Employee 28 as the person who removed that firewall policy during decommissioning [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095, 1094)]. This shows a concrete linkage between a personnel action (policy removal) and service decommissioning; the records provide an auditable trail but also highlight that policy removals should be coordinated with service owners and change control to avoid accidental business impact where dependencies exist [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095)]. ## Firewall logs were useful for correlation but change tracking and template management are weak points Firewall logs revealed the outbound restriction rule and helped correlate the firewall change to incident timing, demonstrating that logging was effective for forensic correlation in this incident [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. However, the fact that a default profile reload replaced a custom template and that a perimeter patch altered templates/policies implies insufficient template/version control and inadequate pre/post‑change validation across firewall and reverse‑proxy/WAF configurations. Strengthening template management, change staging, and automated config drift detection would reduce the chance of template reversion or silent policy overwrites during patches [Data: Entities (310, 303); Relationships (497)]. ## Cross‑site differences (Zurich vs Basel) indicate inconsistent change impact and the need for standardized firewall/NAT voice handling Zurich experienced a clear outbound restriction blocking RTP, while Basel’s ports and NAT entries were validated as standard and correct; this divergence reflects inconsistent change effects across sites and the potential for site‑specific policies or stateful behaviours (NAT/timeouts) to produce different outcomes from the same patch window [Data: Entities (303, 336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. To mitigate similar multi‑site incidents, the organization should standardize voice NAT policies, document expected NAT state/timeout settings for Voice VLANs, and include voice‑specific acceptance tests in patch/change windows to quickly detect media disruption that may not surface in basic port checks [Data: Entities (303, 304); Relationships (502)]. ## Operational risk: weekend/emergency changes with inadequate rollback and coordination increase outage likelihood The incident chain shows emergency security hardening and a weekend perimeter patch coinciding with user‑facing outages (voice no‑audio and 502 errors) and an unintended service decommissioning, indicating that out‑of‑hours changes without robust coordination, rollback, and verification can produce high operational risk [Data: Entities (303, 310, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 1077)]. This supports instituting stricter change windows, mandatory pre‑change impact assessments (including downstream WAF and NAT interactions), retained pre‑patch configuration snapshots, and automated post‑change functional tests (HTTP header checks and RTP/SBC call tests) to ensure rapid detection and rollback when changes have unanticipated effects [Data: Entities (303, 310); Relationships (497)].

context: SOURCE 8: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Incoming calls from specific numbers not received Ticket number: TI-1612036 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 22 Owner: Employee 41 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 18.09.25 08:56 Closure Closure date: 29.09.25 16:18 Closure text: Analysis completed. Routing and translation rules adjusted according to Direct Routing Blueprint. Incoming calls fully functional again. Activities 18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22 Good morning We have noticed that incoming calls from certain customers are no longer reaching us. Callers report that they either receive a busy tone or the connection is disconnected after a few seconds. Other external calls are functioning normally. Could you please check this? Kind regards 18.09.25 08:56 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41. 18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please provide the following information: – Example phone numbers where the issue occurs – Since when the issue has existed – Whether all users are affected or only specific ones Thank you. 18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response Here are two examples: +41 44 785 23 10 +41 44 785 23 11 Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue. The problem has existed since last week. All employees are affected. 18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works. Test with number in specified range → call not delivered. In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown. 19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis Possible provider issue. Carrier contacted. 19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC. No routing errors visible on their side. 22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis For functioning numbers: INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams. For affected numbers: INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”. 22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer Good day We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC. However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally. Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions? Kind regards 22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers. The affected numbers belong to this new range. 23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review Comparison with document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3” Section regarding: • Normalization of incoming numbers • E.164 format • Translation rules on SBC • Voice routing policies Finding: New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table. Routing rule covers only the old number range. 24.09.25 – Test Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range. Test call performed. Call correctly signaled and delivered. Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible. 25.09.25 – Coordination with customer Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated. Internal documentation had not yet been updated. 26.09.25 – Adjustment Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 29.09.25 – Customer feedback All tested numbers are working again. Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls. Thank you for the support. 29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 22 The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again. Activities 22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk Good morning Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds. The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time. Internal Teams calls are not affected. Could you please review this? Kind regards 22.07.25 09:14 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35. 22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Thank you for your report. Could you please confirm: – Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls? – Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration? – Are all locations affected or only a specific one? Thank you very much. 22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response It only affects the Basel location. Both incoming and outgoing calls. The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier. Home office users are not affected. 22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note Test performed with affected user. External call to mobile phone. Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds. No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.” 23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis Possible client error or unstable connection. Test with another device → same behavior. Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change. 23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer Good day We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs. Signaling is established correctly. Could this be relevant? 24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis SBC logs reviewed: – SIP INVITE successful – 200 OK correct – Call established – After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC No obvious error code. 24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis No blocking visible. Ports open according to standard configuration. 24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis Session-Expires header reviewed. Session timer set to 30 seconds. Comparison with other locations shows: There, 180 seconds. 25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation Document: “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1” Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant. Note in the blueprint: For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination. 25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC. Test call performed. Call remains stable for several minutes. Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible. 28.07.25 – Coordination with network team New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy. UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds. Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds. 29.07.25 – Adjustment NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 30.07.25 – Customer feedback Good morning We conducted several test calls. No more disconnections observed. Longer conversations are also stable. Thank you very much. 31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 16 The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Calls now remain stable. REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)]. Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)]. ## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)]. For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. This indicates the fix combined configuration changes and lifecycle operations (restarts), which has implications for maintenance windows, availability planning, and test validation; these operational steps should be captured in a repeatable runbook to avoid regressions or unexpected downtime in future rollouts. ## Operational and governance implications: tooling, change control, and runbook coverage The data establishes a concise chain: IISCRYPTO (tool) → BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES (configuration) → IIS SERVERS (infrastructure) → ticket closure (TI-722511) [Data: Entities (18), (20), (26); Relationships (34), (35), (9)]. Investigations should collect latency and packet-path telemetry from representative clients to each app server (including 001-SRV-AOS72/73), and from app servers to Data Mart/SQL. Correlate latency spikes or retransmissions with user-reported slowdowns and with the times FRDB resets or configuration changes occurred to identify whether network factors coincidentally or causally contributed to the incidents. Escalating to Microsoft suggests the internal team reached a boundary where vendor insights (for the reporting product/runtime, TLS negotiation behaviors, or AOS interactions) were needed, and that the incident may involve platform-level behavior or documented product bugs. This necessitates a clear escalation and evidence package (repro steps, diagnostic logs from 72/73, TLS traces, service-account access failures, FRDB snapshots) to reduce time-to-resolution with the vendor. Maintain a central incident bundle and a named internal owner to coordinate evidence collection and vendor interactions to avoid duplicated effort and message fragmentation. Additionally, IISCRYPTO is explicitly associated with modifying IIS servers, confirming it is the operational mechanism for this change rather than a manual registry edit or alternative tool [Data: Relationships (35); Entities (18), (26)]. This identifies IISCRYPTO as a key operational control that should be included in configuration management, testing procedures, and any automation/orchestration workflows for TLS configuration. ## IIS servers are the target infrastructure and part of the web/application tier The IIS SERVERS entity is described as the target of the IISCRYPTO configuration changes and is noted as part of the web/application server tier alongside AOS servers [Data: Relationships (35); Entities (26)]. The support remediation was to create/adjust a Conditional Access policy to selectively allow SMTP AUTH for impacted users (notably the Sales team), restoring send functionality while attempting to preserve the tenant security posture. [Data: Entities (585, 586, 608, 587); Relationships (1015, 1019, 1020)] ## Root cause: Security Defaults disabled SMTP AUTH and broke legacy Outlook mail-sending Enabling the tenant Security Default policy had the direct effect of disabling legacy authentication flows (including SMTP AUTH), which is identified as the root cause of send failures for legacy Outlook clients [Data: Entities (586, 585), Relationships (1019, 1015)]. The symptom reported for clients was a specific SMTP authentication failure: '535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful' with messages left in the Outbox, tying the protocol-level failure (SMTP AUTH) to the user-visible impact in Outlook (Build 2019 MSI) [Data: Entities (585), Relationships (1015)]. ## Affected asset class: legacy Outlook (Build 2019 MSI) relied on SMTP AUTH Legacy Outlook clients (explicitly referenced as Build 2019 MSI) used SMTP AUTH to authenticate to the SMTP server for sending mail; when SMTP AUTH was disabled, those clients were unable to authenticate and send mail, producing the authentication error and queued Outbox items [Data: Relationships (1015), Entities (585)]. This identifies a clear device/application dependency: certain desktop mail clients remain dependent on legacy protocol support, so tenant-wide security changes that disable legacy auth will directly impact those clients until they are updated or excepted. ## Remediation chosen: Conditional Access policy used to selectively re-enable legacy auth Support adjusted Conditional Access to selectively permit SMTP AUTH for affected users/clients as the operational remediation, explicitly noting the change allowed legacy SMTP authentication for the Sales team while aiming to maintain the tenant security posture [Data: Entities (587), Relationships (1020)]. This demonstrates an in-place mitigation approach that uses modern access controls (Conditional Access policies) to scope exceptions rather than reverting tenant-wide Security Defaults, enabling business continuity for impacted users without fully relaxing the tenant security posture [Data: Entities (587, 586), Relationships (1020, 1019)]. ## Security/operational trade-off: scoped exception vs. broad exposure The chosen approach acknowledges a trade-off: re-enabling legacy SMTP AUTH for a subset of users restores functionality but increases exposure to legacy-auth risks. The documentation indicates the CA change was made to 'selectively allow SMTP AUTH' (i.e., scoped exception) rather than disable Security Defaults tenant-wide [Data: Entities (587, 586), Relationships (1020, 1019)]. Because legacy authentication is a recognized category distinct from modern auth in the corpus, the existence of that category (Legacy Authentication) reinforces the need for careful exception scoping, monitoring, and a migration plan to reduce long-term risk [Data: Entities (608)]. ## Operational visibility and troubleshooting: clear protocol-level error enables targeted remediation The presence of a precise error ('535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful') and the mapping between client type and protocol (Outlook 2019 MSI -> SMTP AUTH) provided a tight diagnostic signal that permitted a targeted remediation (Conditional Access exception) rather than broad rollback [Data: Entities (585), Relationships (1015, 1020)]. This level of diagnostic clarity supports reproducibility of the troubleshooting steps and allows teams to document a focused runbook: detect SMTP AUTH failures in legacy clients, validate tenant Security Defaults state, and implement scoped CA exceptions where necessary [Data: Entities (585, 586, 587), Relationships (1015, 1019, 1020)]. ## Governance implications and recommended follow-up actions Because the incident stems from an intentional tenant-level security setting (Security Defaults) conflicting with legacy client behavior, follow-up governance actions are indicated: inventory legacy-auth-dependent clients, define an exception approval process for Conditional Access, monitor usage of re-enabled legacy auth, and plan migration to modern authentication. These recommended controls are consistent with the remediation path chosen (selective CA exceptions) and the conceptual Legacy Authentication entity describing older auth methods [Data: Entities (586, 587, 608), Relationships (1020, 1019)]. Documenting the exception criteria and adding monitoring/alerting for SMTP AUTH use will reduce the chance of silent regressions if Security Defaults change again or if additional clients begin failing. The recorded relationships between Security Defaults, Legacy Authentication, SMTP AUTH, and Conditional Access provide the basis for these governance controls and runbooks [Data: Entities (586, 608, 585, 587), Relationships (1019, 1020, 1015)].

[Data: Relationships (1021, 1025, 1026, 1028)] Medium-term (1–3 months): update change governance to require impact assessment and stakeholder notification (Endpoint/Client Support, Exchange/Email Ops, Identity/Security teams, and affected business teams like Sales) before tenant-level authentication policy changes. Add monitoring/alerts for mass 0x800CCC0E occurrences and for SMTP AUTH authentication failures to detect regression quickly. ## Recommended stakeholder matrix and escalation path Based on the entities and relationships in the records, the minimal operational stakeholder map should include: the affected Business Unit (Sales), Endpoint/Client Support (Windows 11 workstation owners and helpdesk), Exchange/Email Operations (SMTP server and submission configuration), and Security/Identity (tenant authentication defaults and policies). Relationship records tie the legacy Outlook client to the Sales team and the Windows 11 endpoints to client firewall checks, which justifies these role mappings. [Data: Relationships (1021, 1029); Entities (588, 589, 599)] Escalation should follow a clear path: Sales user reports → Tier 1 helpdesk (execute client-side runbook: firewall/antivirus/profile checks) → Endpoint/Client Support (if hardware/security controls implicated) → Exchange/Email Ops (validate server-side/authentication and SMTP submission behavior) → Security/Identity (if tenant-level policy change required). Define SLAs for each handoff and ensure change control for any tenant-level exception to SMTP AUTH. ## Side note: Windows 11 endpoint privacy settings are documented but not material to SMTP issue The Windows 11 workstation entity includes a note that privacy settings controlled camera access and blocked camera usage by apps (Teams and browsers). While this is documented in the same endpoint record, it is unrelated to the SMTP/Outlook sending failures described here; it does indicate that endpoint configuration details are being captured in the dataset and could be useful for other operational troubleshooting. [Data: Entities (589)] Maintain separate runbooks for endpoint feature/configuration issues (camera/privacy) and email/authentication issues to avoid conflating unrelated endpoint symptoms during incident triage. REPORT 51: # SMTP AUTH, Security Defaults, Legacy Outlook (Build 2019 MSI) and Conditional Access A tenant-level activation of Security Defaults disabled legacy authentication (including SMTP AUTH), which caused legacy Outlook clients (Build 2019 MSI) to fail SMTP sign-on with '535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful' and left messages in user Outboxes. The support remediation was to create/adjust a Conditional Access policy to selectively allow SMTP AUTH for impacted users (notably the Sales team), restoring send functionality while attempting to preserve the tenant security posture. The core entities are SMTP AUTH (legacy protocol), the Security Default policy (tenant-wide setting), the conceptual Legacy Authentication category, and the Conditional Access policy used for selective exceptions. [Data: Entities (585, 586, 608, 587); Relationships (1015, 1019, 1020)] ## Root cause: Security Defaults disabled SMTP AUTH and broke legacy Outlook mail-sending Enabling the tenant Security Default policy had the direct effect of disabling legacy authentication flows (including SMTP AUTH), which is identified as the root cause of send failures for legacy Outlook clients [Data: Entities (586, 585), Relationships (1019, 1015)]. The symptom reported for clients was a specific SMTP authentication failure: '535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful' with messages left in the Outbox, tying the protocol-level failure (SMTP AUTH) to the user-visible impact in Outlook (Build 2019 MSI) [Data: Entities (585), Relationships (1015)]. ## Affected asset class: legacy Outlook (Build 2019 MSI) relied on SMTP AUTH Legacy Outlook clients (explicitly referenced as Build 2019 MSI) used SMTP AUTH to authenticate to the SMTP server for sending mail; when SMTP AUTH was disabled, those clients were unable to authenticate and send mail, producing the authentication error and queued Outbox items [Data: Relationships (1015), Entities (585)]. This identifies a clear device/application dependency: certain desktop mail clients remain dependent on legacy protocol support, so tenant-wide security changes that disable legacy auth will directly impact those clients until they are updated or excepted. ## Remediation chosen: Conditional Access policy used to selectively re-enable legacy auth Support adjusted Conditional Access to selectively permit SMTP AUTH for affected users/clients as the operational remediation, explicitly noting the change allowed legacy SMTP authentication for the Sales team while aiming to maintain the tenant security posture [Data: Entities (587), Relationships (1020)]. This demonstrates an in-place mitigation approach that uses modern access controls (Conditional Access policies) to scope exceptions rather than reverting tenant-wide Security Defaults, enabling business continuity for impacted users without fully relaxing the tenant security posture [Data: Entities (587, 586), Relationships (1020, 1019)]. ## Security/operational trade-off: scoped exception vs. broad exposure The chosen approach acknowledges a trade-off: re-enabling legacy SMTP AUTH for a subset of users restores functionality but increases exposure to legacy-auth risks. The documentation indicates the CA change was made to 'selectively allow SMTP AUTH' (i.e., scoped exception) rather than disable Security Defaults tenant-wide [Data: Entities (587, 586), Relationships (1020, 1019)]. Because legacy authentication is a recognized category distinct from modern auth in the corpus, the existence of that category (Legacy Authentication) reinforces the need for careful exception scoping, monitoring, and a migration plan to reduce long-term risk [Data: Entities (608)]. ## Operational visibility and troubleshooting: clear protocol-level error enables targeted remediation The presence of a precise error ('535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful') and the mapping between client type and protocol (Outlook 2019 MSI -> SMTP AUTH) provided a tight diagnostic signal that permitted a targeted remediation (Conditional Access exception) rather than broad rollback [Data: Entities (585), Relationships (1015, 1020)]. This level of diagnostic clarity supports reproducibility of the troubleshooting steps and allows teams to document a focused runbook: detect SMTP AUTH failures in legacy clients, validate tenant Security Defaults state, and implement scoped CA exceptions where necessary [Data: Entities (585, 586, 587), Relationships (1015, 1019, 1020)]. ## Governance implications and recommended follow-up actions Because the incident stems from an intentional tenant-level security setting (Security Defaults) conflicting with legacy client behavior, follow-up governance actions are indicated: inventory legacy-auth-dependent clients, define an exception approval process for Conditional Access, monitor usage of re-enabled legacy auth, and plan migration to modern authentication. These recommended controls are consistent with the remediation path chosen (selective CA exceptions) and the conceptual Legacy Authentication entity describing older auth methods [Data: Entities (586, 587, 608), Relationships (1020, 1019)]. Documenting the exception criteria and adding monitoring/alerting for SMTP AUTH use will reduce the chance of silent regressions if Security Defaults change again or if additional clients begin failing. The recorded relationships between Security Defaults, Legacy Authentication, SMTP AUTH, and Conditional Access provide the basis for these governance controls and runbooks [Data: Entities (586, 608, 585, 587), Relationships (1019, 1020, 1015)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 8: # SharePoint folder access — Sensitivity Label vs ACL (Customer Company 25 AG / Compliance_Review_2025) This community centers on a recurring SharePoint access problem in Customer Company 25 AG where a folder (/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025) was granted unique SharePoint ACLs, then later had a Microsoft Information Protection sensitivity label applied. The label enforced encryption tied to a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) while the SharePoint ACL referenced SP_Compliance_Main (which in turn referenced a cloud-created COMPLIANCE_TEAM). Azure AD Connect synchronization of an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM object with the same display name (different ObjectID) replaced or changed the cloud object evaluation, excluding cloud-only external guests and producing access loss. Token caching and token-claim timing caused the symptom to appear intermittent (users could temporarily access with stale tokens, then lose access when tokens refreshed). The issue was reproduced in a TEST TENANT, investigated using Azure AD sign-in logs and Microsoft Purview, escalated through HELPDESK → BU Infrastructure (M365/Identity) → System Engineers, remediated by group consolidation / full sync / reapply label / invalidate tokens, and validated by the customer's Audit Team and Consulting Sample Company. ## Primary technical root cause: label encryption permissions diverged from SharePoint ACLs because the label was applied after unique permissions and 'Preserve custom permissions' was not enabled The folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 had Unique Permissions (a SharePoint ACL) applied, and the Sensitivity Label ("Confidential – Internal") was applied later; because the label was applied without enabling the "Preserve custom permissions" option, the label's encryption referenced a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) than the ACL did, producing a permissions divergence that prevented decryption for some users. This sequence (ACL → label) and the missing preserve option are explicitly documented in the incident analysis and relationships examined during the investigation. This misalignment meant that SharePoint could show an ACL granting access (SP_Compliance_Main) while the label-based encryption check denied decryption because the token claims did not show membership in Compliance_Core. The label-enforced encryption thus became the effective gatekeeper for content access in practice, overriding the expectation set by the ACL alone. The incident record links the folder, the label configuration and the preserve-permissions option directly to the observed inconsistent access behavior. [Data: Entities (550, 571, 575, 556); Relationships (981, 947, 980)] ## Azure AD Connect group/object collision excluded external guests when the synchronized On‑Prem group object took precedence Investigators found a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (containing external guest accounts) and a separate On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (with the same display name but a different Object ID). Azure AD Connect synchronization behavior caused the synchronized On‑Prem group object to overwrite or replace the cloud object reference used by SharePoint, which excluded cloud-only guest members and caused external guests to lose access when SharePoint evaluated the synchronized group object. System Engineer 14 and the identity team reproduced and explained this behaviour using Microsoft Graph queries and by reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior. The remediation included consolidation and a full sync to restore the intended cloud group membership. This synchronization/ID collision is the proximate cause that converted the ACL/label divergence into an actual user outage for external guests. [Data: Entities (269, 265, 266, 267); Relationships (446, 437, 430)] ## Token caching and claim timing produced intermittent/partial access symptoms Multiple records indicate that cached or previously-issued authentication tokens contained older group claims (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) that temporarily allowed access until tokens were refreshed or re-evaluated — at which point the absence of the Compliance_Core claim caused decryption to fail. Browser session state (including Incognito vs normal sessions) and Office client sessions retained tokens that influenced who saw the folder and when. Azure AD sign-in logs and token/claim records showed affected tokens contained COMPLIANCE_TEAM claims but lacked Compliance_Core; sign-in log timing and Conditional Access evaluation events correlated with observed intermittent behavior. Engineers therefore combined group/object fixes with token invalidation guidance (full sign‑out from Office + browser) to force reissuance of tokens with current claims. The cached-token behavior explains the seemingly sporadic nature of the access failures. [Data: Entities (681, 570, 558, 574); Relationships (991, 962, 1204)] ## Issue was reproducible in an isolated TEST TENANT and client-session tests showed cache-related differences Engineers reproduced the same ACL→label sequence in a TEST TENANT by creating a folder with unique permissions and applying a Sensitivity Label after the ACL was set; the test confirmed that token renewal and timing produced the same temporary-allowed/then-denied behavior. Browser Incognito testing produced different/partial access compared to normal sessions, further implicating cached session tokens and client caching as modifiers of symptom presentation. The use of a non-production test tenant allowed safe reproduction of the sequence and validation of remediation steps before applying them to production, and test findings (including incognito behavior) were used to justify the combined remediation approach (group consolidation + full sync + token invalidation + label reapplication). [Data: Entities (503, 567, 262); Relationships (993, 955, 996)] ## This is a recurrent, multi-ticket operational problem with demonstrated business impact Multiple support incidents across March–April 2025 reference similar SharePoint access issues for Customer Company 25 AG (tickets TI-1571046, TI-1578891, TI-1583327) and a related Teams voice ticket for a different customer (TI-1609823). The Customer Company 25 AG incidents specifically documented external project members losing access to project folders and required escalation to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and system engineers for in‑depth remediation. The recurrence and the number of escalations indicate a systemic interaction between label application processes, group/object management and synchronization rules that goes beyond a single, transient misconfiguration — and therefore warrants process and architecture changes to prevent repeat outages. [Data: Entities (546, 256, 676, 189); Relationships (419, 934, 1180, 288)] ## Stakeholder and escalation pathways were established and executed, but clarified ownership of label application and group governance is needed The support workflow shows HELPDESK as the frontline intake and triage point, escalating to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and then to System Engineers 11 and 14 for deeper analysis; Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and remediation and the customer's Audit Team validated the final state. Communication paths (helpdesk ↔ contact persons, identity BU ↔ system engineers, consulting vendor ↔ customer) were used effectively to resolve the incidents. However, the root cause — interplay between SharePoint ACLs, sensitivity label configuration, and Azure AD group/object management — crosses service, identity and compliance boundaries. This cross-domain ownership means that explicit, codified governance is required for label application (who may apply labels and how), group naming/object‑ID collision handling, and Azure AD Connect change control in order to avoid reintroducing the same fault through routine administrative changes. [Data: Entities (85, 549, 261, 548, 568, 572); Relationships (297, 944, 967, 971)] ## Security/compliance configuration nuance: labels enforced encryption but ACL/label misalignment can deny intended principals Sensitivity Labels managed through Microsoft Purview/AIP enforce encryption that is evaluated against Azure AD group membership (e.g., requiring membership in Compliance_Core). The label mechanism is therefore capable of blocking decryption even when SharePoint ACLs indicate permission. In this incident, applying the label without preserving custom ACL mapping effectively changed the authority model for access, producing potential availability impacts for legitimate users and creating a risk that compliance-related labels could be misapplied in ways that disrupt business processes. The investigation used Purview tooling to review label configuration and concluded that label application options (such as "Preserve custom permissions") materially affect access outcomes; thus label application must be treated as both a security and availability control, and require pre‑application checks and a rollback/test path. [Data: Entities (557, 559, 556); Relationships (977, 959, 981)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to prevent recurrence Based on the documented causes and remediation steps, the following prioritized actions are indicated: (1) Treat label application as a controlled change — enable "Preserve custom permissions" or explicitly map ACL → label group mappings and test in a TEST TENANT prior to production; (2) Implement Azure AD Connect governance: detect display-name collisions, require ObjectID reconciliation/explicit mapping for groups with identical display names, and use pre-sync validation to prevent cloud group overwrite; (3) Add a runbook to force token revocation and clear Office/browser sessions after any group or label change (sign‑out guidance, token revocation, and customer communication); (4) Maintain a pre-label checklist (verify group referenced by label exists and contains intended principals including guests) and require Purview review/approval for applied encryption labels; (5) Add monitoring/alerts for rapid detection: track label application events, group-object replacements, and anomalous sign-in errors referencing encryption failures. Each recommended action maps directly to evidence and remediation steps recorded in the incident (preserve permissions option, Azure AD Connect behavior, test-tenant verification, token invalidation guidance, and Purview analysis) and should be implemented in the order of operational impact and ease of deployment (preserve-permissions / pre-application tests first, then AAD Connect governance and automation). [Data: Entities (575, 269, 503, 681, 559); Relationships (980, 446, 993, 1204, 940)] REPORT 23: # NDES / AD CS Certificate Services & Device Authentication Community This community centers on the Certificate Services & Device Authentication blueprint that documents deployment, operation, troubleshooting, and escalation for Microsoft NDES integrated with Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) and Intune-managed device certificates. The blueprint (authoritative guidance) contains a decision tree for technicians, explicit monitoring recommendations, and enumerated failure modes; AD CS (Root and Issuing CA) is a core backend dependency whose misconfiguration or expiry causes authentication failures or certificate issuance failures. The decision tree explicitly routes unresolved issuance problems to Infrastructure/AD CS specialists as the escalation target. Recommended controls in the blueprint include separating Root and Issuing CA roles, implementing monitoring of certificate requests and errors, validating IIS/service-account/AD CS post-configuration steps, and creating clear escalation/runbook paths for persistent issuance faults. [Data: Entities (359,361,372); Relationships (579,596,583,607)] ## Blueprint is the authoritative operational guide for NDES and certificate-based device authentication The document titled 'CERTIFICATE SERVICES & DEVICE AUTHENTICATION (MICROSOFT NDES / CERTIFICATES / INTUNE / AD CS)' is presented as the blueprint covering design, decision-tree guidance, best practices, installation/troubleshooting steps, and operational guidance for NDES and the certificate integration points with Intune and AD CS. It explicitly describes NDES roles, failure modes, and stepwise remediation actions, making it the single consolidated reference for technicians deploying or operating this capability. Because the blueprint bundles decision-tree checks, monitoring guidance, and remediation steps (IIS components, AD CS post-configuration, service account settings, CA/template selection), it should be treated as the authoritative runbook for first- and second-line responders and as the basis for formalizing escalation and change processes for certificate issuance services. [Data: Entities (359); Relationships (579,583,372)] ## Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) is a single, high-impact dependency for NDES AD CS provides Root and Issuing CA roles, certificate templates, and lifecycle management and is explicitly described as the backend that NDES depends on; AD CS post-configuration is required for NDES to operate. Failures or misconfigurations in AD CS (expired CA certs, wrong CA selection, misconfigured templates) are identified as causes of authentication failures, connection drops, or inability to issue device certificates. Because AD CS issues and lifecycle-management are upstream of NDES, any operational or security change to the CA hierarchy affects device authentication broadly; therefore AD CS must be treated as a critical service with dedicated monitoring, change control, and escalation ownership. [Data: Entities (361); Relationships (596,600)] ## Decision tree encodes operational checks and prescribes escalation to infrastructure specialists The 'DECISION TREE – NDES INSTALLATION & OPERATION' contains a structured troubleshooting flow (seven verification steps) covering reachability, NDES service status, IIS components, AD CS post-configuration, service account configuration, CA/template selection, and final issuance testing—providing a reproducible first-line diagnostic workflow for technicians. When the decision tree’s checks do not resolve issuance failures, it explicitly directs escalation to Infrastructure/AD CS specialists as the next-level support for deeper PKI, CA, and server remediation; this creates a clear handoff path from NDES operators to specialists. Operationalizing that handoff (contacts, SLA, and triage criteria) will reduce time-to-resolution for persistent certificate issuance problems. [Data: Entities (372,374); Relationships (607,600)] ## Monitoring of certificate requests and issuance errors is recommended and critical to detect indirect service impacts The blueprint contains a dedicated operational guidance item 'MONITORING OF CERTIFICATE REQUESTS AND ERRORS' that recommends tracking issuance requests, errors, expiries, and communication failures between NDES, AD CS, and target systems as a best practice. It notes that certificate problems often manifest indirectly as authentication failures or connection drops, so targeted monitoring is needed to surface these root causes early. Implementing the monitoring guidance will enable proactive alerts for expiring CA or device certificates, repeated template- or issuance-errors, and communication failures between NDES and AD CS—allowing teams to remediate before business-impacting authentication outages occur. [Data: Entities (371); Relationships (583,579)] ## Common failure modes and remediation areas are well-documented and should be converted into testable runbooks The blueprint enumerates common failure modes including expired or invalid certificates, misconfigured templates, disrupted communication paths between NDES and AD CS, missing IIS components, incorrect service account settings, and improper CA/template selection—these are the primary operational causes for certificate issuance failures described in the document. Each listed failure mode maps to concrete verification and remediation steps in the decision tree; converting those steps into short, testable runbooks (with verification checks and telemetry/alert thresholds) will improve first-contact resolution and make escalation to infrastructure specialists more efficient. [Data: Entities (359,372,361)] ## Infrastructure/AD CS specialists are identified as the escalation target but need formalized responsibilities Infrastructure specialists (including AD CS specialists) are identified explicitly as the escalation target when NDES cannot issue certificates after initial troubleshooting; the decision tree and blueprint point to them for deeper PKI and server-level remediation. This clarifies who must be engaged for triage beyond the decision-tree steps. However, the presence of an identified escalation target in the blueprint is not the same as fully operationalized ownership—teams should formalize contact information, SLA expectations, required log/trace artifacts to collect prior to escalation, and regular cross-training sessions so that the escalation path yields consistent outcomes. [Data: Entities (374); Relationships (607,600)] ## Separation of Root and Issuing CA roles is recommended to reduce PKI risk The blueprint and AD CS description recommend clear separation between Root CA and Issuing CA roles to meet best practices and minimize risk to the PKI. This separation reduces blast radius from a compromised issuing CA and supports more secure key custodianship and certificate lifecycle controls. Implementing and documenting a separated CA hierarchy, along with associated access controls, backup/restore procedures, and validation tests, should be treated as a priority security control for the certificate services community. [Data: Entities (361); Relationships (596)] ## Blueprint contains built-in recommendations that enable prioritized remediation actions Because the blueprint already includes a decision tree, monitoring guidance, a list of common failure modes, and escalation targets, it provides sufficient content to prioritize near-term operational improvements: (1) implement monitoring/alerts for issuance-errors and expiry, (2) enforce CA role separation and validate AD CS post-configuration, (3) convert decision-tree checks into formal runbooks and pre-escalation checklists, and (4) formalize escalation SLAs and contact matrices with Infrastructure specialists. These actionable items can be implemented with low‑to‑moderate effort and are directly supported by the blueprint content—making them high‑value first steps to reduce certificate-related outages and security exposures. [Data: Entities (269, 265, 266, 267); Relationships (446, 437, 430)] ## Token caching and claim timing produced intermittent/partial access symptoms Multiple records indicate that cached or previously-issued authentication tokens contained older group claims (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) that temporarily allowed access until tokens were refreshed or re-evaluated — at which point the absence of the Compliance_Core claim caused decryption to fail. Browser session state (including Incognito vs normal sessions) and Office client sessions retained tokens that influenced who saw the folder and when. Azure AD sign-in logs and token/claim records showed affected tokens contained COMPLIANCE_TEAM claims but lacked Compliance_Core; sign-in log timing and Conditional Access evaluation events correlated with observed intermittent behavior. Engineers therefore combined group/object fixes with token invalidation guidance (full sign‑out from Office + browser) to force reissuance of tokens with current claims. The cached-token behavior explains the seemingly sporadic nature of the access failures. [Data: Entities (681, 570, 558, 574); Relationships (991, 962, 1204)] ## Issue was reproducible in an isolated TEST TENANT and client-session tests showed cache-related differences Engineers reproduced the same ACL→label sequence in a TEST TENANT by creating a folder with unique permissions and applying a Sensitivity Label after the ACL was set; the test confirmed that token renewal and timing produced the same temporary-allowed/then-denied behavior. Browser Incognito testing produced different/partial access compared to normal sessions, further implicating cached session tokens and client caching as modifiers of symptom presentation. The use of a non-production test tenant allowed safe reproduction of the sequence and validation of remediation steps before applying them to production, and test findings (including incognito behavior) were used to justify the combined remediation approach (group consolidation + full sync + token invalidation + label reapplication). [Data: Entities (503, 567, 262); Relationships (993, 955, 996)] ## This is a recurrent, multi-ticket operational problem with demonstrated business impact Multiple support incidents across March–April 2025 reference similar SharePoint access issues for Customer Company 25 AG (tickets TI-1571046, TI-1578891, TI-1583327) and a related Teams voice ticket for a different customer (TI-1609823). The Customer Company 25 AG incidents specifically documented external project members losing access to project folders and required escalation to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and system engineers for in‑depth remediation. The recurrence and the number of escalations indicate a systemic interaction between label application processes, group/object management and synchronization rules that goes beyond a single, transient misconfiguration — and therefore warrants process and architecture changes to prevent repeat outages. [Data: Entities (546, 256, 676, 189); Relationships (419, 934, 1180, 288)] ## Stakeholder and escalation pathways were established and executed, but clarified ownership of label application and group governance is needed The support workflow shows HELPDESK as the frontline intake and triage point, escalating to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and then to System Engineers 11 and 14 for deeper analysis; Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and remediation and the customer's Audit Team validated the final state. Communication paths (helpdesk ↔ contact persons, identity BU ↔ system engineers, consulting vendor ↔ customer) were used effectively to resolve the incidents. However, the root cause — interplay between SharePoint ACLs, sensitivity label configuration, and Azure AD group/object management — crosses service, identity and compliance boundaries. This cross-domain ownership means that explicit, codified governance is required for label application (who may apply labels and how), group naming/object‑ID collision handling, and Azure AD Connect change control in order to avoid reintroducing the same fault through routine administrative changes. [Data: Entities (85, 549, 261, 548, 568, 572); Relationships (297, 944, 967, 971)] ## Security/compliance configuration nuance: labels enforced encryption but ACL/label misalignment can deny intended principals Sensitivity Labels managed through Microsoft Purview/AIP enforce encryption that is evaluated against Azure AD group membership (e.g., requiring membership in Compliance_Core). The label mechanism is therefore capable of blocking decryption even when SharePoint ACLs indicate permission. In this incident, applying the label without preserving custom ACL mapping effectively changed the authority model for access, producing potential availability impacts for legitimate users and creating a risk that compliance-related labels could be misapplied in ways that disrupt business processes. The investigation used Purview tooling to review label configuration and concluded that label application options (such as "Preserve custom permissions") materially affect access outcomes; thus label application must be treated as both a security and availability control, and require pre‑application checks and a rollback/test path. [Data: Entities (557, 559, 556); Relationships (977, 959, 981)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to prevent recurrence Based on the documented causes and remediation steps, the following prioritized actions are indicated: (1) Treat label application as a controlled change — enable "Preserve custom permissions" or explicitly map ACL → label group mappings and test in a TEST TENANT prior to production; (2) Implement Azure AD Connect governance: detect display-name collisions, require ObjectID reconciliation/explicit mapping for groups with identical display names, and use pre-sync validation to prevent cloud group overwrite; (3) Add a runbook to force token revocation and clear Office/browser sessions after any group or label change (sign‑out guidance, token revocation, and customer communication); (4) Maintain a pre-label checklist (verify group referenced by label exists and contains intended principals including guests) and require Purview review/approval for applied encryption labels; (5) Add monitoring/alerts for rapid detection: track label application events, group-object replacements, and anomalous sign-in errors referencing encryption failures. Each recommended action maps directly to evidence and remediation steps recorded in the incident (preserve permissions option, Azure AD Connect behavior, test-tenant verification, token invalidation guidance, and Purview analysis) and should be implemented in the order of operational impact and ease of deployment (preserve-permissions / pre-application tests first, then AAD Connect governance and automation). [Data: Entities (575, 269, 503, 681, 559); Relationships (980, 446, 993, 1204, 940)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes.
