Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit.

The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules. REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)]. REPORT 100: # SAP Print Servers — VRINFRGERPRNT03 / VRINFRGERPRNT04 Incident Community This community centers on the SAP print infrastructure (logical group: SAP PRINT SERVERS) and two dedicated Windows VM print servers, VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04, which failed to process SAP print jobs during a post‑maintenance window. Operators attempted service-level remediation (Windows Print Spooler restart) and then a VM rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot; printing remained broken and both VMs experienced blue screens (BSOD) during post‑restore validation. The incident activated the Infrastructure On‑Call team and involved hands‑on restoration actions with named restore contexts (INTER12203 / INTER12204) and an individual identified as Mister X. A suspected driver/OS/Windows update regression is explicitly called out by stakeholders as a likely root cause. Immediate operational impact is confined to SAP printing on the affected servers but the failure mode—OS instability after rollback—indicates deeper platform or configuration risk that requires coordinated diagnostics, controlled validation, and governance changes to prevent recurrence. [Data: Entities (545, 154, 155); Relationships (913, 907, 914, 908, 911 +more)] ## SAP print cluster and two VM members are the operational center of the incident The logical service affected is the SAP print infrastructure; VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 are explicitly identified as members of the SAP PRINT SERVERS group and were implicated in the failure to deliver SAP print jobs. These two VMs are the primary production endpoints for SAP-generated print output and therefore central to business printing continuity. [Data: Entities (545, 154, 155); Relationships (913, 907, 914)] Because both VMs exhibited the same failure pattern (printing stopped, spooler restarts attempted, then rollback and subsequent crashes), the failure is likely systemic to how these servers are configured or to recent platform changes rather than an isolated queue or application issue. This increases the urgency for cross‑layer diagnostics (OS, drivers, hypervisor/host, and SAP print subsystem). [Data: Entities (154, 155); Relationships (907, 914)] ## Persistent symptom pattern: spooler restart and VM restart did not remediate; BSODs occurred after snapshot rollback Operators performed typical first‑line remediation—restarting the Windows Print Spooler and rebooting the VMs—but these actions did not restore SAP printing functionality. The print spooler restart is recorded as an action (Employee 23) and is associated with the affected servers. [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (921, 907, 914)] Following unsuccessful service restarts, a rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot was executed; during subsequent validation both VMs experienced blue screens (BSOD), indicating kernel/driver/OS instability rather than a transient spooler process fault. The appearance of BSODs after a restore suggests the underlying fault either persisted across snapshots or was triggered by the restoration sequence, requiring deeper forensic analysis (memory dumps, event logs, driver lists, and host/hypervisor checks). [Data: Entities (542, 154, 155); Relationships (911, 908)] ## Windows update / OS version is the suspected root cause and rollback was requested Stakeholders explicitly linked the incident to Windows updates or the OS version and requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, indicating a plausible regression introduced by a recent update. This suspicion is recorded as a request by Contact Person 11 to roll back Windows versioning on the print servers. [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (923)] While a Windows update is a credible lead, the fact that BSODs appeared after a snapshot rollback implies either a driver incompatibility carried in the snapshot or a host/VM mismatch with updated components; the hypothesis should be validated with patch/version inventories, update timelines, and comparison of pre/post maintenance driver and system component lists. [Data: Entities (154, 155); Relationships (908, 923)] ## Recovery actions and actors: Infrastructure On‑Call and named restore contexts were engaged The incident was escalated to the Infrastructure On‑Call team, which authorized and coordinated a rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot (17.07 21:00) as a high‑priority recovery action. Hands‑on restoration was performed in the context of identifiers INTER12203 and INTER12204 and involved a technician referenced as Mister X. These actors and contexts should be captured in an incident timeline and runbook for future reproducibility and accountability. [Data: Entities (538, 539, 540, 541); Relationships (908, 924, 925)] Recording the specific restore hosts/contexts and the technicians involved is valuable for post‑incident review: it enables tracing of the precise restore procedure, the source snapshot used, and any host‑level conditions that may have influenced the outcome (for example, host patch level, VM driver compatibility, or snapshot consistency). [Data: Relationships (908, 924, 925)] ## Operational validation and change control gaps evidenced by BSODs after restore The occurrence of blue screens during validation after a rollback highlights insufficient controlled post‑restore validation and possibly gaps in the change control process for Windows updates or VM snapshots. The documented restore to pre‑maintenance and the subsequent BSODs on at least one server are recorded in the incident logs. [Data: Relationships (908, 911); Entities (154, 155)] This pattern suggests the need to strengthen pre/post maintenance validation checklists (including driver signature/compatibility checks, automated health checks, and staged rollouts) and to mandate capture of crash dumps and hypervisor logs immediately after any restore that precedes system instability. Procedural improvements should be tied to the Infrastructure On‑Call workflow so restores are executed under a standardized validation plan. [Data: Entities (538); Relationships (908, 911)] ## Stakeholder communication and ownership are partially recorded but could be clearer There is evidence of collaborative work and internal acknowledgements—Employee 19 took over analysis of BSOD and printing issues and sent internal recognition for contributors—indicating some ownership and handoff actions during the incident. However, responsibility for update rollbacks, forensic analysis, and post‑restore validation is not fully codified in the recorded artifacts. [Data: Entities (516); Relationships (879)] Additionally, an explicit recorded action (Employee 23 restarting the print spooler) shows first‑line troubleshooting occurred, but the logs do not clearly document definitive assignment for root‑cause remediation (who will pursue driver rollbacks, who will gather crash dumps, who will coordinate with application owners). Formalizing owner roles (service owner, platform owner, on‑call lead) in the runbook would reduce ambiguity in future incidents. [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (921, 879)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to reduce recurrence and speed remediation 1) Immediate forensic tasks: collect kernel crash dumps (BSOD) and system event logs from VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04, and archive the exact snapshot used for rollback for controlled replay; this is supported by the occurrence of BSODs after restore and the recorded restore action. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 542); Relationships (908, 911)] 2) Validate Windows update hypothesis: perform a controlled rollback of the Windows update/version on a non‑production clone and compare behavior, and inventory recent patches applied to the print servers as requested by Contact Person 11. [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (923)] 3) Strengthen post‑maintenance validation and change control: implement a mandatory post‑restore validation checklist (including driver compatibility checks and health probes) and require Infrastructure On‑Call sign‑off before marking print services as recovered. The restore and on‑call activation events provide the operational trigger points for this control. [Data: Entities (538); Relationships (908)] 4) Update runbooks & escalation: codify ownership for print service incidents (service owner, platform owner, on‑call lead), document restore contexts (INTER12203 / INTER12204) and performer (Mister X) for auditability, and add an escalation ladder for failures that persist after service restart. Existing records of restore contexts and actors should be used to populate runbook templates. [Data: Entities (539, 540, 541); Relationships (924, 925)] 5) Staged rollout and driver management: before broad Windows updates on print VMs, test updates against a staging snapshot of the SAP print servers and maintain a validated list of approved print drivers; given that spooler restarts did not resolve the issue and BSODs followed, driver/OS compatibility is a high‑priority risk to mitigate. [Data: Entities (536, 542, 154); Relationships (907, 911)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].

REPORT 29: # AD FS Identity & Authentication Community (AD FS, MFA, Claims Providers) This community centers on an on‑premises AD FS deployment acting as the primary authentication and token issuance service, with dependent components including MFA, external claims providers, client registrations (OIDC/SAML), target applications/integrations, certificate/token signing, a decision‑tree troubleshooting guide, and logging/monitoring systems. AD FS issues either SAML or JWT/OIDC tokens depending on client registration parameters and can either enforce MFA itself or delegate it to claims providers; those integration choices alter authentication flows, logging visibility, and operational reliability for browser and non‑browser clients. The Decision Tree and Identity/Architecture team provide structured diagnostics and escalation paths for unresolved authentication issues, while logging/monitoring are described as required for post‑remediation validation and for tracing complex flows that cross AD FS and external claims providers. [Data: Entities (208,212,213,217,219,225), Relationships (330,336,322,337,341)] ## AD FS is the documented central authentication authority and the primary subject for configuration and troubleshooting The blueprint explicitly identifies Active Directory Federation Services (AD FS) as the central on‑premises identity provider responsible for token issuance, client registrations, certificate management, and where MFA integration decisions are applied [Data: Entities (208)]. Operational guidance repeatedly ties diagnostic actions and remediation steps to AD FS configuration, showing it as the focal point for resolving authentication failures across browser and non‑browser flows (for example, token format selection and client registration parameters). This centrality is reinforced by its connection to logging/monitoring for event capture and by relationships to token formats (JWT/OIDC) the service can issue [Data: Relationships (330,324)]. ## Delegating MFA to external claims providers increases flow complexity and operational dependencies The document describes two MFA approaches: enforcing MFA directly in AD FS or delegating MFA to external claims providers; when delegation is used, the external claims providers become part of the authentication path and must be available and trusted for successful sign‑ons [Data: Entities (212,213)]. Because MFA is executed by claims providers in the delegated model, tracing authentication and MFA events requires correlated logs across AD FS and the claims providers, and the blueprint calls out monitoring both sides to diagnose timeouts, errors, and claim transformations. This relationship creates additional points of failure and increases the need for cross‑system observability and SLAs for claims providers [Data: Relationships (336,337)]. ## Divergent behavior between browser and non‑browser logins is a recurring diagnostic pattern The blueprint contrasts browser‑based interactive flows (where SSO and interactive MFA typically work) with non‑browser service/API flows that are noninteractive, noting that browser logins may succeed while integrations fail due to differing client parameters and token format support [Data: Entities (214,215)]. This distinction is formalized in the Decision Tree's troubleshooting logic and the relationships linking non‑browser logins to applications/integrations; the document explicitly points investigators to check client registration flags, token formats, and protocol differences when non‑browser flows fail but browser flows do not [Data: Relationships (338,339,322)]. ## Incorrect client registration parameters and token‑format mismatches are common root causes for application failures Client registration artifacts (Client ID, Redirect URI and related flags) are called out as frequent sources of authentication failure for target applications and integrations; the blueprint emphasizes documenting and verifying these registrations as a key governance and troubleshooting control [Data: Entities (217)]. Similarly, when AD FS is configured to emit JWT/OIDC tokens, target applications and APIs must be able to validate those tokens; lack of support or misconfigured validation leads to non‑browser integration failures. The relationships tying AD FS, token formats, client registration parameters, and applications highlight this failure mode and its remediation path (confirm registration values, expected token format, and application validation logic) [Data: Relationships (341,334,324,334)]. ## Logging and monitoring are treated as required control points for diagnostics and post‑remediation assurance The blueprint prescribes capturing Azure AD sign‑in and sync logs, AD FS token issuance and claims events, client error codes/correlation IDs, and change/audit trails for membership and directory sync operations to enable forensic tracing and to validate remediation outcomes [Data: Entities (219)]. Operational notes indicate active monitoring of the environment following remediation to confirm that group consolidations and Azure AD Connect synchronizations resolved inconsistencies; when MFA is delegated to external providers, logs from both AD FS and the claims providers are necessary to reconstruct flows and confirm successful MFA events [Data: Relationships (330,337)]. ## The Decision Tree codifies triage steps and explicit escalation to the Identity/Architecture team A Decision Tree (Questions 1–10) is provided to triage authentication issues by distinguishing browser vs non‑browser, protocol (OIDC/JWT vs SAML), client registration checks, MFA delegation status, and certificate/time checks—serving as a first‑line operational runbook [Data: Entities (225), Relationships (322)]. The Decision Tree also defines escalation criteria and routes unresolved or complex issues to the Identity/Architecture team for deeper analysis of AD FS configuration, client registrations, and certificate issues, making the Identity/Architecture team an explicit escalation target in the operational design [Data: Entities (220), Relationships (348)]. ## Certificates and token signing key management are a highlighted recurring governance risk The blueprint calls out certificates and token signing keys as areas requiring periodic review for expiry, correct signature validation, and time‑skew checks, and recommends these checks as part of root‑cause analysis for intermittent authentication errors [Data: Entities (218)]. Certificate lifecycle and signature validation escalation responsibilities are assigned to the Identity/Architecture team, indicating that lapses in certificate management or token‑signing key rotations can lead to widespread authentication impact and require architecture‑level remediation [Data: Relationships (346)]. ## Clear stakeholder responsibilities exist but demand active coordination and documented handoffs The blueprint identifies application owners as the responsible stakeholders for target applications and requires them to confirm client registration values and expected token formats; this creates a necessary touchpoint for validating application‑side changes during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (221), Relationships (342,343)]. At the same time, the Identity/Architecture team is the escalation and governance owner for AD FS configuration, certificates, and deeper analysis; the presence of explicit escalation paths and decision tree steps shows a designed operational handoff but also implies a need for documented SLAs and coordination processes (for example, between application owners, the identity team, and external claims providers) to avoid prolonged outages or repeated incidents [Data: Relationships (348,343,336)].

A weekend security patch and an emergency security‑hardening change caused template/profile reloads and an outbound restriction: the perimeter change activated a default inspection profile (Default‑Strict) on the reverse‑proxy/WAF which replaced a custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling producing 502s; separately, an outbound restriction at the Zurich edge blocked RTP media to the SBC and produced no audio for external calls. Basel’s site showed a standard port configuration and correct port 443/NAT entries, pointing to NAT/state timeout interactions rather than simple port blocking as the cause of voice degradation. An employee‑recorded removal of a firewall policy on 17.12.24 resulted in decommissioning of externally‑accessible FTP. The corpus provides logs and change links that correlate these configuration changes with observed service failures. [Data: Entities (303, 310, 336, 304, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 502, 541, 1095)] ## Reverse‑proxy/WAF profile reload (Default‑Strict) replaced custom template and caused header handling regressions and 502s A security patch/reload resulted in the default inspection/profile "Default‑Strict" being activated on the reverse‑proxy/WAF, which replaced the previously used custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling; this change is directly linked by ticket notes to 502 errors and header removal observed after the patch [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. The records show a template/profile-level change on the reverse‑proxy/WAF rather than an application‑level code change, indicating configuration drift at the perimeter layer is responsible for application‑facing HTTP errors; this makes template management and configuration rollback capability primary risk/mitigation points for restoring service quickly after future patches [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. ## Emergency outbound restriction at Zurich blocked RTP to SBC and caused no audio for external calls Investigation by the Network Team identified an emergency security‑hardening change at the Zurich edge that introduced a new outbound restriction blocking client RTP ports to the Session Border Controller (SBC), which produced no audio for users calling external parties [Data: Entities (303)]. Firewall logs corroborated the timing and presence of the outbound restriction rule, supporting a causal link between the firewall change and the onset of audio issues; the logs were pivotal in correlating change time to the incident start time [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. ## Basel site: standard port rules present; NAT and session/timeout behavior implicated rather than port blocking Checks at the Basel site validated the standard port configuration and confirmed that port 443 rules and NAT entries were present and correct, demonstrating that simple port closures were not the cause of service regression there [Data: Entities (336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. Because ports and NAT entries appeared correct, the analysis points to NAT/state timeout interactions (or asymmetric NAT behaviour) as contributing factors to voice impact, indicating a need to review NAT timeouts and session persistence for Voice VLAN/NAT policies to prevent intermittent media failures under patched or hardened firewall states [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (502, 541)]. ## Perimeter firewall patch produced configuration drift affecting reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior The perimeter firewall received a security patch/upgrade over the same weekend that—despite logs not showing obvious drops—overwrote or altered templates/policies and caused configuration drift that affected reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior; this is noted explicitly in the firewall analysis summary [Data: Entities (303)]. The combination of silent template/policy changes on perimeter firewalls and their downstream effects on WAF/reverse‑proxy headers creates a high‑impact failure mode because application layer failures (502s, header loss) can appear unrelated to the original network/security change without clear cross‑team change visibility [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (497)]. ## External FTP decommissioning was performed by an identified employee removing the governing firewall policy An externally‑accessible FTP service footprint was decommissioned on 17.12.24 when the firewall policy governing it was removed; ticket evidence records Employee 28 as the person who removed that firewall policy during decommissioning [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095, 1094)]. This shows a concrete linkage between a personnel action (policy removal) and service decommissioning; the records provide an auditable trail but also highlight that policy removals should be coordinated with service owners and change control to avoid accidental business impact where dependencies exist [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095)]. ## Firewall logs were useful for correlation but change tracking and template management are weak points Firewall logs revealed the outbound restriction rule and helped correlate the firewall change to incident timing, demonstrating that logging was effective for forensic correlation in this incident [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. However, the fact that a default profile reload replaced a custom template and that a perimeter patch altered templates/policies implies insufficient template/version control and inadequate pre/post‑change validation across firewall and reverse‑proxy/WAF configurations. Strengthening template management, change staging, and automated config drift detection would reduce the chance of template reversion or silent policy overwrites during patches [Data: Entities (310, 303); Relationships (497)]. ## Cross‑site differences (Zurich vs Basel) indicate inconsistent change impact and the need for standardized firewall/NAT voice handling Zurich experienced a clear outbound restriction blocking RTP, while Basel’s ports and NAT entries were validated as standard and correct; this divergence reflects inconsistent change effects across sites and the potential for site‑specific policies or stateful behaviours (NAT/timeouts) to produce different outcomes from the same patch window [Data: Entities (303, 336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. To mitigate similar multi‑site incidents, the organization should standardize voice NAT policies, document expected NAT state/timeout settings for Voice VLANs, and include voice‑specific acceptance tests in patch/change windows to quickly detect media disruption that may not surface in basic port checks [Data: Entities (303, 304); Relationships (502)]. ## Operational risk: weekend/emergency changes with inadequate rollback and coordination increase outage likelihood The incident chain shows emergency security hardening and a weekend perimeter patch coinciding with user‑facing outages (voice no‑audio and 502 errors) and an unintended service decommissioning, indicating that out‑of‑hours changes without robust coordination, rollback, and verification can produce high operational risk [Data: Entities (303, 310, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 1077)]. This supports instituting stricter change windows, mandatory pre‑change impact assessments (including downstream WAF and NAT interactions), retained pre‑patch configuration snapshots, and automated post‑change functional tests (HTTP header checks and RTP/SBC call tests) to ensure rapid detection and rollback when changes have unanticipated effects [Data: Entities (303, 310); Relationships (497)].

REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].
