Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 93: # Perimeter Firewall, Reverse‑Proxy/WAF & Voice NAT (Zurich/Basel) This community centers on perimeter and site firewalls, a reverse‑proxy/WAF, NAT rules for published services, and an externally‑accessible FTP footprint that was decommissioned. A weekend security patch and an emergency security‑hardening change caused template/profile reloads and an outbound restriction: the perimeter change activated a default inspection profile (Default‑Strict) on the reverse‑proxy/WAF which replaced a custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling producing 502s; separately, an outbound restriction at the Zurich edge blocked RTP media to the SBC and produced no audio for external calls. Basel’s site showed a standard port configuration and correct port 443/NAT entries, pointing to NAT/state timeout interactions rather than simple port blocking as the cause of voice degradation. An employee‑recorded removal of a firewall policy on 17.12.24 resulted in decommissioning of externally‑accessible FTP. The corpus provides logs and change links that correlate these configuration changes with observed service failures. [Data: Entities (303, 310, 336, 304, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 502, 541, 1095)] ## Reverse‑proxy/WAF profile reload (Default‑Strict) replaced custom template and caused header handling regressions and 502s A security patch/reload resulted in the default inspection/profile "Default‑Strict" being activated on the reverse‑proxy/WAF, which replaced the previously used custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling; this change is directly linked by ticket notes to 502 errors and header removal observed after the patch [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. The records show a template/profile-level change on the reverse‑proxy/WAF rather than an application‑level code change, indicating configuration drift at the perimeter layer is responsible for application‑facing HTTP errors; this makes template management and configuration rollback capability primary risk/mitigation points for restoring service quickly after future patches [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. ## Emergency outbound restriction at Zurich blocked RTP to SBC and caused no audio for external calls Investigation by the Network Team identified an emergency security‑hardening change at the Zurich edge that introduced a new outbound restriction blocking client RTP ports to the Session Border Controller (SBC), which produced no audio for users calling external parties [Data: Entities (303)]. Firewall logs corroborated the timing and presence of the outbound restriction rule, supporting a causal link between the firewall change and the onset of audio issues; the logs were pivotal in correlating change time to the incident start time [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. ## Basel site: standard port rules present; NAT and session/timeout behavior implicated rather than port blocking Checks at the Basel site validated the standard port configuration and confirmed that port 443 rules and NAT entries were present and correct, demonstrating that simple port closures were not the cause of service regression there [Data: Entities (336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. Because ports and NAT entries appeared correct, the analysis points to NAT/state timeout interactions (or asymmetric NAT behaviour) as contributing factors to voice impact, indicating a need to review NAT timeouts and session persistence for Voice VLAN/NAT policies to prevent intermittent media failures under patched or hardened firewall states [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (502, 541)]. ## Perimeter firewall patch produced configuration drift affecting reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior The perimeter firewall received a security patch/upgrade over the same weekend that—despite logs not showing obvious drops—overwrote or altered templates/policies and caused configuration drift that affected reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior; this is noted explicitly in the firewall analysis summary [Data: Entities (303)]. The combination of silent template/policy changes on perimeter firewalls and their downstream effects on WAF/reverse‑proxy headers creates a high‑impact failure mode because application layer failures (502s, header loss) can appear unrelated to the original network/security change without clear cross‑team change visibility [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (497)]. ## External FTP decommissioning was performed by an identified employee removing the governing firewall policy An externally‑accessible FTP service footprint was decommissioned on 17.12.24 when the firewall policy governing it was removed; ticket evidence records Employee 28 as the person who removed that firewall policy during decommissioning [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095, 1094)]. This shows a concrete linkage between a personnel action (policy removal) and service decommissioning; the records provide an auditable trail but also highlight that policy removals should be coordinated with service owners and change control to avoid accidental business impact where dependencies exist [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095)]. ## Firewall logs were useful for correlation but change tracking and template management are weak points Firewall logs revealed the outbound restriction rule and helped correlate the firewall change to incident timing, demonstrating that logging was effective for forensic correlation in this incident [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. However, the fact that a default profile reload replaced a custom template and that a perimeter patch altered templates/policies implies insufficient template/version control and inadequate pre/post‑change validation across firewall and reverse‑proxy/WAF configurations. Strengthening template management, change staging, and automated config drift detection would reduce the chance of template reversion or silent policy overwrites during patches [Data: Entities (310, 303); Relationships (497)]. ## Cross‑site differences (Zurich vs Basel) indicate inconsistent change impact and the need for standardized firewall/NAT voice handling Zurich experienced a clear outbound restriction blocking RTP, while Basel’s ports and NAT entries were validated as standard and correct; this divergence reflects inconsistent change effects across sites and the potential for site‑specific policies or stateful behaviours (NAT/timeouts) to produce different outcomes from the same patch window [Data: Entities (303, 336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. To mitigate similar multi‑site incidents, the organization should standardize voice NAT policies, document expected NAT state/timeout settings for Voice VLANs, and include voice‑specific acceptance tests in patch/change windows to quickly detect media disruption that may not surface in basic port checks [Data: Entities (303, 304); Relationships (502)]. ## Operational risk: weekend/emergency changes with inadequate rollback and coordination increase outage likelihood The incident chain shows emergency security hardening and a weekend perimeter patch coinciding with user‑facing outages (voice no‑audio and 502 errors) and an unintended service decommissioning, indicating that out‑of‑hours changes without robust coordination, rollback, and verification can produce high operational risk [Data: Entities (303, 310, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 1077)]. This supports instituting stricter change windows, mandatory pre‑change impact assessments (including downstream WAF and NAT interactions), retained pre‑patch configuration snapshots, and automated post‑change functional tests (HTTP header checks and RTP/SBC call tests) to ensure rapid detection and rollback when changes have unanticipated effects [Data: Entities (303, 310); Relationships (497)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].

Additionally, IISCRYPTO is explicitly associated with modifying IIS servers, confirming it is the operational mechanism for this change rather than a manual registry edit or alternative tool [Data: Relationships (35); Entities (18), (26)]. This identifies IISCRYPTO as a key operational control that should be included in configuration management, testing procedures, and any automation/orchestration workflows for TLS configuration. ## IIS servers are the target infrastructure and part of the web/application tier The IIS SERVERS entity is described as the target of the IISCRYPTO configuration changes and is noted as part of the web/application server tier alongside AOS servers [Data: Relationships (35); Entities (26)]. Because the IIS servers are in the application tier, changes to their TLS configuration have potential reach to client compatibility, web application availability, and upstream/downstream integration points; the records therefore identify these servers as an important operational domain for TLS policy enforcement and verification. ## Remediation included activation of recommended cipher suites and server restarts The BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES record documents that activation of recommended TLS cipher suites, performed with IISCRYPTO and accompanied by server restarts, resolved the issue described in the ticket [Data: Entities (20); Relationships (9); Relationships (34)]. This indicates the fix combined configuration changes and lifecycle operations (restarts), which has implications for maintenance windows, availability planning, and test validation; these operational steps should be captured in a repeatable runbook to avoid regressions or unexpected downtime in future rollouts. ## Operational and governance implications: tooling, change control, and runbook coverage The data establishes a concise chain: IISCRYPTO (tool) → BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES (configuration) → IIS SERVERS (infrastructure) → ticket closure (TI-722511) [Data: Entities (18), (20), (26); Relationships (34), (35), (9)]. From a governance and operations perspective this implies three priorities: include IISCRYPTO and the exact cipher profile in formal change requests and configuration baselines; ensure pre-change compatibility testing and post-change validation (including automation where viable); and codify the combined configuration + restart sequence in runbooks so incidents tied to TLS/cipher issues can be resolved reproducibly without ad-hoc intervention. The presence of a ticket-based remediation shows change control was exercised for this case, but the documented tool-and-target relationships suggest these items should be standardized across the server fleet. ## Potential client-compatibility and testing considerations tied to cipher hardening The records show the environment moved to recommended/best-practice TLS cipher suites on IIS servers using IISCRYPTO [Data: Entities (20), (18), (26); Relationships (34), (35)]. While the dataset does not record any compatibility failures, any systematic hardening of cipher suites can impact legacy clients or integrations that depend on older ciphers or protocol versions; therefore, change plans should include explicit client-compatibility testing, stakeholder notification, and rollback criteria. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 93: # Perimeter Firewall, Reverse‑Proxy/WAF & Voice NAT (Zurich/Basel) This community centers on perimeter and site firewalls, a reverse‑proxy/WAF, NAT rules for published services, and an externally‑accessible FTP footprint that was decommissioned. A weekend security patch and an emergency security‑hardening change caused template/profile reloads and an outbound restriction: the perimeter change activated a default inspection profile (Default‑Strict) on the reverse‑proxy/WAF which replaced a custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling producing 502s; separately, an outbound restriction at the Zurich edge blocked RTP media to the SBC and produced no audio for external calls. Basel’s site showed a standard port configuration and correct port 443/NAT entries, pointing to NAT/state timeout interactions rather than simple port blocking as the cause of voice degradation. An employee‑recorded removal of a firewall policy on 17.12.24 resulted in decommissioning of externally‑accessible FTP. The corpus provides logs and change links that correlate these configuration changes with observed service failures. [Data: Entities (303, 310, 336, 304, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 502, 541, 1095)] ## Reverse‑proxy/WAF profile reload (Default‑Strict) replaced custom template and caused header handling regressions and 502s A security patch/reload resulted in the default inspection/profile "Default‑Strict" being activated on the reverse‑proxy/WAF, which replaced the previously used custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling; this change is directly linked by ticket notes to 502 errors and header removal observed after the patch [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. The records show a template/profile-level change on the reverse‑proxy/WAF rather than an application‑level code change, indicating configuration drift at the perimeter layer is responsible for application‑facing HTTP errors; this makes template management and configuration rollback capability primary risk/mitigation points for restoring service quickly after future patches [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. ## Emergency outbound restriction at Zurich blocked RTP to SBC and caused no audio for external calls Investigation by the Network Team identified an emergency security‑hardening change at the Zurich edge that introduced a new outbound restriction blocking client RTP ports to the Session Border Controller (SBC), which produced no audio for users calling external parties [Data: Entities (303)]. Firewall logs corroborated the timing and presence of the outbound restriction rule, supporting a causal link between the firewall change and the onset of audio issues; the logs were pivotal in correlating change time to the incident start time [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. ## Basel site: standard port rules present; NAT and session/timeout behavior implicated rather than port blocking Checks at the Basel site validated the standard port configuration and confirmed that port 443 rules and NAT entries were present and correct, demonstrating that simple port closures were not the cause of service regression there [Data: Entities (336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. Because ports and NAT entries appeared correct, the analysis points to NAT/state timeout interactions (or asymmetric NAT behaviour) as contributing factors to voice impact, indicating a need to review NAT timeouts and session persistence for Voice VLAN/NAT policies to prevent intermittent media failures under patched or hardened firewall states [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (502, 541)]. ## Perimeter firewall patch produced configuration drift affecting reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior The perimeter firewall received a security patch/upgrade over the same weekend that—despite logs not showing obvious drops—overwrote or altered templates/policies and caused configuration drift that affected reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior; this is noted explicitly in the firewall analysis summary [Data: Entities (303)]. The combination of silent template/policy changes on perimeter firewalls and their downstream effects on WAF/reverse‑proxy headers creates a high‑impact failure mode because application layer failures (502s, header loss) can appear unrelated to the original network/security change without clear cross‑team change visibility [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (497)]. ## External FTP decommissioning was performed by an identified employee removing the governing firewall policy An externally‑accessible FTP service footprint was decommissioned on 17.12.24 when the firewall policy governing it was removed; ticket evidence records Employee 28 as the person who removed that firewall policy during decommissioning [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095, 1094)]. This shows a concrete linkage between a personnel action (policy removal) and service decommissioning; the records provide an auditable trail but also highlight that policy removals should be coordinated with service owners and change control to avoid accidental business impact where dependencies exist [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095)]. ## Firewall logs were useful for correlation but change tracking and template management are weak points Firewall logs revealed the outbound restriction rule and helped correlate the firewall change to incident timing, demonstrating that logging was effective for forensic correlation in this incident [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. However, the fact that a default profile reload replaced a custom template and that a perimeter patch altered templates/policies implies insufficient template/version control and inadequate pre/post‑change validation across firewall and reverse‑proxy/WAF configurations. Strengthening template management, change staging, and automated config drift detection would reduce the chance of template reversion or silent policy overwrites during patches [Data: Entities (310, 303); Relationships (497)]. ## Cross‑site differences (Zurich vs Basel) indicate inconsistent change impact and the need for standardized firewall/NAT voice handling Zurich experienced a clear outbound restriction blocking RTP, while Basel’s ports and NAT entries were validated as standard and correct; this divergence reflects inconsistent change effects across sites and the potential for site‑specific policies or stateful behaviours (NAT/timeouts) to produce different outcomes from the same patch window [Data: Entities (303, 336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. To mitigate similar multi‑site incidents, the organization should standardize voice NAT policies, document expected NAT state/timeout settings for Voice VLANs, and include voice‑specific acceptance tests in patch/change windows to quickly detect media disruption that may not surface in basic port checks [Data: Entities (303, 304); Relationships (502)]. ## Operational risk: weekend/emergency changes with inadequate rollback and coordination increase outage likelihood The incident chain shows emergency security hardening and a weekend perimeter patch coinciding with user‑facing outages (voice no‑audio and 502 errors) and an unintended service decommissioning, indicating that out‑of‑hours changes without robust coordination, rollback, and verification can produce high operational risk [Data: Entities (303, 310, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 1077)]. This supports instituting stricter change windows, mandatory pre‑change impact assessments (including downstream WAF and NAT interactions), retained pre‑patch configuration snapshots, and automated post‑change functional tests (HTTP header checks and RTP/SBC call tests) to ensure rapid detection and rollback when changes have unanticipated effects [Data: Entities (303, 310); Relationships (497)].
